is a mathematically defined surface that approximates the [geoid](/wiki/Geoid "Geoid"), which is the truer, imperfect [figure of the Earth](/wiki/Figure_of_the_Earth "Figure of the Earth"), or other planetary body, as opposed to a perfect, smooth, and unaltered sphere, which factors in the undulations of the bodies' [gravity](/wiki/Gravity "Gravity") due to variations in the composition and density of the [interior](/wiki/Planetary_core "Planetary core"), as well as the subsequent [flattening](/wiki/Flattening "Flattening") caused by the [centrifugal force](/wiki/Centrifugal_force "Centrifugal force") from the rotation of these massive objects (for planetary bodies that do rotate).

An **Earth ellipsoid** or **Earth spheroid** is a mathematical figure approximating the [Earth's shape and size](/wiki/Figure_of_the_Earth "Figure of the Earth"), used as a [reference frame](/wiki/Frame_of_reference "Frame of reference") for computations in [geodesy](/wiki/Geodesy "Geodesy"), [astronomy](/wiki/Astronomy "Astronomy"), and the [geosciences](/wiki/Geoscience "Geoscience"). Various different [reference ellipsoids](/wiki/Reference_ellipsoid "Reference ellipsoid") have been used as approximations.

It is an [oblate spheroid](/wiki/Oblate_spheroid "Oblate spheroid") (an ellipsoid of [revolution](/wiki/Surface_of_revolution "Surface of revolution")) whose [minor axis](/wiki/Minor_axis "Minor axis") (polar diameter), connecting the [geographical poles](/wiki/Geographical_pole "Geographical pole"), is approximately aligned with the [Earth's axis of rotation](/wiki/Earth%27s_axis_of_rotation "Earth's axis of rotation"). The ellipsoid is also defined by the major axis (*equatorial axis*); the difference between the two axes is slightly more than 21 km or 0.335%.

Many methods exist for determination of the axes of an Earth ellipsoid, ranging from [meridian arcs](/wiki/Meridian_arc "Meridian arc") up to modern [satellite geodesy](/wiki/Satellite_geodesy "Satellite geodesy") or the analysis and interconnection of continental [geodetic networks](/wiki/Geodetic_network "Geodetic network"). Amongst the different set of data used in [national surveys](/wiki/National_survey "National survey") are several of special importance: the [Bessel ellipsoid](/wiki/Bessel_ellipsoid "Bessel ellipsoid") of 1841, the international [Hayford ellipsoid](/wiki/Hayford_ellipsoid "Hayford ellipsoid") of 1924, and (for [GPS](/wiki/GPS "GPS") positioning) the [WGS84](/wiki/WGS84 "WGS84") ellipsoid.

## Types

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Earth_ellipsoid&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Types")]

There are two types of ellipsoid: mean and reference.

A data set which describes the global [average](/wiki/Average "Average") of the Earth's surface curvature is called the *mean Earth Ellipsoid*. It refers to a theoretical coherence between the [geographic latitude](/wiki/Geographic_latitude "Geographic latitude") and the meridional curvature of the [geoid](/wiki/Geoid "Geoid"). The latter is close to the [mean sea level](/wiki/Mean_sea_level "Mean sea level"), and therefore an ideal Earth ellipsoid has the same [volume](/wiki/Volume "Volume") as the geoid.

While the mean Earth ellipsoid is the ideal basis of global geodesy, for [regional](/wiki/Regional "Regional") networks a so-called *reference ellipsoid* may be the better choice.[[1]](#cite_note-1) When geodetic measurements have to be computed on a mathematical reference surface, this surface should have a similar curvature as the regional geoid; otherwise, [reduction](/wiki/Reduction_(mathematics) "Reduction (mathematics)") of the measurements will get small distortions.

This is the reason for the "long life" of former reference ellipsoids like the [Hayford](/wiki/Hayford_ellipsoid "Hayford ellipsoid") or the [Bessel ellipsoid](/wiki/Bessel_ellipsoid "Bessel ellipsoid"), despite the fact that their main axes deviate by several hundred meters from the modern values. Another reason is a judicial one: the [coordinates](/wiki/Coordinate "Coordinate") of millions of boundary stones should remain fixed for a long period. If their reference surface changes, the coordinates themselves also change.

However, for international networks, [GPS](/wiki/GPS "GPS") positioning, or [astronautics](/wiki/Astronautics "Astronautics"), these regional reasons are less relevant. As knowledge of [the Earth's figure](/wiki/Figure_of_the_Earth "Figure of the Earth") is increasingly accurate, the International Geoscientific Union [IUGG](/wiki/IUGG "IUGG") usually adapts the axes of the Earth ellipsoid to the best available data.

### Reference ellipsoid

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Earth_ellipsoid&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Reference ellipsoid")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b5/OblateSpheroid.PNG/250px-OblateSpheroid.PNG)](/wiki/File:OblateSpheroid.PNG)

Flattened sphere

In [geodesy](/wiki/Geodesy "Geodesy"), a **reference ellipsoid** is a mathematically defined surface that approximates the [geoid](/wiki/Geoid "Geoid"), which is the truer, imperfect [figure of the Earth](/wiki/Figure_of_the_Earth "Figure of the Earth"), or other planetary body, as opposed to a perfect, smooth, and unaltered sphere, which factors in the undulations of the bodies' [gravity](/wiki/Gravity "Gravity") due to variations in the composition and density of the [interior](/wiki/Planetary_core "Planetary core"), as well as the subsequent [flattening](/wiki/Flattening "Flattening") caused by the [centrifugal force](/wiki/Centrifugal_force "Centrifugal force") from the rotation of these massive objects (for planetary bodies that do rotate).
Because of their relative simplicity, reference ellipsoids are used as a preferred surface on which [geodetic network](/wiki/Geodetic_network "Geodetic network") computations are performed and point coordinates such as [latitude](/wiki/Latitude "Latitude"), [longitude](/wiki/Longitude "Longitude"), and [elevation](/wiki/Elevation "Elevation") are defined.

In the context of standardization and geographic applications, a *geodesic reference ellipsoid* is the mathematical model used as foundation by [spatial reference system](/wiki/Spatial_reference_system "Spatial reference system") or [geodetic datum](/wiki/Geodetic_datum "Geodetic datum") definitions.

## Ellipsoid parameters

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Earth_ellipsoid&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Ellipsoid parameters")]

In geophysics, [geodesy](/wiki/Geodesy "Geodesy"), and related areas, the word 'ellipsoid' is understood to mean 'oblate ellipsoid of revolution', and the older term 'oblate spheroid' is hardly used.[[2]](#cite_note-torge-2)[[3]](#cite_note-flattening-3) For bodies that cannot be well approximated by an ellipsoid of revolution a [triaxial](/wiki/Triaxial_ellipsoid "Triaxial ellipsoid") (or scalene) ellipsoid is used.

The shape of an ellipsoid of revolution is determined by the shape parameters of that [ellipse](/wiki/Ellipse "Ellipse"). The [semi-major axis](/wiki/Semi-major_axis "Semi-major axis") of the ellipse, a, becomes the equatorial radius of the ellipsoid: the [semi-minor axis](/wiki/Semi-minor_axis "Semi-minor axis") of the ellipse, b, becomes the distance from the centre to either pole. These two lengths completely specify the shape of the ellipsoid.

In geodesy publications, however, it is common to specify the semi-major axis (equatorial radius) a and the [flattening](/wiki/Flattening "Flattening") f, defined as:

:   ![{\displaystyle f={\frac {a-b}{a}}.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/8caff55e7069e9a3421cac91fbdad7f2290a72c8)

That is, f is the amount of flattening at each pole, relative to the radius at the equator. This is often expressed as a fraction 1/m; *m* = 1/*f* then being the "inverse flattening". A great many other [ellipse parameters](/wiki/Ellipse "Ellipse") are used in [geodesy](/wiki/Geodesy "Geodesy") but they can all be related to one or two of the set a, b and f.

A great many ellipsoids have been used to model the Earth in the past, with different assumed values of a and b as well as different assumed positions of the center and different axis orientations relative to the solid Earth. Starting in the late twentieth century, improved measurements of satellite orbits and star positions have provided extremely accurate determinations of the Earth's center of mass and of its axis of revolution; and those parameters have been adopted also for all modern reference ellipsoids.

The ellipsoid [WGS-84](/wiki/World_Geodetic_System "World Geodetic System"), widely used for mapping and [satellite navigation](/wiki/GPS "GPS") has f close to 1/300 (more precisely, 1/298.257223563, by definition), corresponding to a difference of the major and minor semi-axes of approximately 21 km (13 miles) (more precisely, 21.3846857548205 km). For comparison, Earth's [Moon](/wiki/Moon "Moon") is even less elliptical, with a flattening of less than 1/825, while [Jupiter](/wiki/Jupiter "Jupiter") is visibly oblate at about 1/15 and one of [Saturn's](/wiki/Saturn "Saturn") triaxial moons, [Telesto](/wiki/Telesto_(moon) "Telesto (moon)"), is highly flattened, with f between 1/3 and 1/2 (meaning that the polar diameter is between 50% and 67% of the equatorial.

## Determination

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Earth_ellipsoid&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Determination")]

[Arc measurement](/wiki/Arc_measurement "Arc measurement") is the historical method of determining the ellipsoid.
Two meridian arc measurements will allow the derivation of two parameters required to specify a reference ellipsoid.
For example, if the measurements were hypothetically performed exactly over the equator plane and either geographical pole, the radii of curvature so obtained would be related to the equatorial radius and the polar radius, respectively *a* and *b* (see: [Earth polar and equatorial radius of curvature](/wiki/Earth_radius#Polar_and_equatorial_radius_of_curvature "Earth radius")). Then, the [flattening](/wiki/Flattening "Flattening") would readily follow from its definition:

:   ![{\displaystyle f=(a-b)/a}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/4de0a2fdb7997262ccee47e4c12df72c818f614b).

For two arc measurements each at arbitrary average latitudes ![{\displaystyle \varphi _{i}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/70503774fb21be77396899900d3aa1e47d8f9e10), ![{\displaystyle i=1,\,2}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/b033edb174b4a30020ee32d81f208f657d691003), the solution starts from an initial approximation for the equatorial radius ![{\displaystyle a_{0}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/693ad9f934775838bd72406b41ada4a59785d7ba) and for the flattening ![{\displaystyle f_{0}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/6423b30a4c5770c59b5ab92dcb4ce378755440ab). The theoretical [Earth's meridional radius of curvature](/wiki/Earth%27s_meridional_radius_of_curvature "Earth's meridional radius of curvature") ![{\displaystyle M_{0}(\varphi _{i})}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/4d2878daf36ce572c292680c9ba88d9309b8aa0f) can be calculated at the latitude of each arc measurement as:

:   ![{\displaystyle M_{0}(\varphi _{i})={\frac {a(1-e^{2})}{(1-e_{0}^{2}\sin ^{2}\varphi _{i})^{\frac {3}{2}}}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/8732d4ce5946ecefdcf790db7e8b66a20cc95271)

where ![{\displaystyle e_{0}^{2}=2f_{0}-f_{0}^{2}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/d3dba662147121a56fc675aeddac4847460621e9).[[4]](#cite_note-4)
Then discrepancies between empirical and theoretical values of the radius of curvature can be formed as ![{\displaystyle \delta M_{i}=M_{i}-M_{0}(\varphi _{i})}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/7a7cdcaa8b62876ac945692d9ecfca70be0f6790). Finally, corrections for the initial equatorial radius ![{\displaystyle \delta a}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/07620d93350410230aaf367409b16945b4817781) and the flattening ![{\displaystyle \delta f}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/6c10972e3d8cc0def199b8f39ad05bb75ddfdc00) can be solved by means of a [system of linear equations](/wiki/System_of_linear_equations "System of linear equations") formulated via [linearization](/wiki/Linearization "Linearization") of ![{\displaystyle M}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/f82cade9898ced02fdd08712e5f0c0151758a0dd):[[5]](#cite_note-Bomford1052-5)

:   ![{\displaystyle \delta M_{i}\approx \delta a(\partial M/\partial a)+\delta f(\partial M/\partial f)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/ad46d1d20af9c491ef75bc139c2df42336d6b49e)

where the partial derivatives are:[[5]](#cite_note-Bomford1052-5)

:   ![{\displaystyle \partial M/\partial a\approx 1}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/dbaf3500d96f3a0a141e55384b8f0c22eb3de710)
:   ![{\displaystyle \partial M/\partial f\approx -2a_{0}(1-1.5\sin ^{2}\varphi _{i})}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/28cd8faaa055c76fbf6a887e79d8b0e00196b435)

Longer arcs with multiple intermediate-latitude determinations can completely determine the ellipsoid that best fits the surveyed region. In practice, multiple arc measurements are used to determine the ellipsoid parameters by the method of [least squares adjustment](/wiki/Least_squares_adjustment "Least squares adjustment"). The parameters determined are usually the semi-major axis, ![{\displaystyle a}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/ffd2487510aa438433a2579450ab2b3d557e5edc), and any of the semi-minor axis, ![{\displaystyle b}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/f11423fbb2e967f986e36804a8ae4271734917c3), [flattening](/wiki/Flattening "Flattening"), or eccentricity.

Regional-scale [systematic effects](/wiki/Systematic_effect "Systematic effect") observed in the radius of curvature measurements reflect the [geoid undulation](/wiki/Geoid_undulation "Geoid undulation") and the [deflection of the vertical](/wiki/Deflection_of_the_vertical "Deflection of the vertical"), as explored in [astrogeodetic leveling](/wiki/Astrogeodetic_leveling "Astrogeodetic leveling").

[Gravimetry](/wiki/Gravimetry "Gravimetry") is another technique for determining Earth's flattening, as per [Clairaut's theorem](/wiki/Clairaut%27s_theorem_(gravity) "Clairaut's theorem (gravity)").

Modern [geodesy](/wiki/Geodesy "Geodesy") no longer uses simple meridian arcs or ground triangulation networks, but the methods of [satellite geodesy](/wiki/Satellite_geodesy "Satellite geodesy"), especially [satellite gravimetry](/wiki/Satellite_gravimetry "Satellite gravimetry").

## Geodetic coordinates

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Earth_ellipsoid&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Geodetic coordinates")]

This section is an excerpt from [Geodetic coordinates](/wiki/Geodetic_coordinates "Geodetic coordinates").[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Geodetic_coordinates&action=edit)]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8f/Geodetic_coordinates.svg/250px-Geodetic_coordinates.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Geodetic_coordinates.svg)

Geodetic coordinates P(*ɸ*,*λ*,*h*)

[Geodetic coordinates](/wiki/Geodetic_coordinates "Geodetic coordinates") are a type of [curvilinear](/wiki/Curvilinear_coordinate_system "Curvilinear coordinate system") [orthogonal coordinate system](/wiki/Orthogonal_coordinate_system "Orthogonal coordinate system") used in [geodesy](/wiki/Geodesy "Geodesy") based on a *[reference ellipsoid](/wiki/Reference_ellipsoid "Reference ellipsoid")*.
They include geodetic latitude (north/south) ϕ, *[longitude](/wiki/Longitude "Longitude")* (east/west) λ, and ellipsoidal height h (also known as geodetic height[[6]](#cite_note-Geodetic_coordinates_National_Geodetic_Survey_(U.S.)._National_Geodetic_Survey_(U.S.)_1986_p._107-6)).
The triad is also known as Earth ellipsoidal coordinates[[7]](#cite_note-Geodetic_coordinates_Awange_Grafarend_Paláncz_Zaletnyik_2010_p._156-7) (not to be confused with *[ellipsoidal-harmonic coordinates](/wiki/Ellipsoidal-harmonic_coordinates "Ellipsoidal-harmonic coordinates")*).

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Earth_ellipsoid&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: History")]

In 1687 [Isaac Newton](/wiki/Isaac_Newton "Isaac Newton") published the [Principia](/wiki/Philosophi%C3%A6_Naturalis_Principia_Mathematica "Philosophiæ Naturalis Principia Mathematica") in which he included a proof that a rotating self-gravitating fluid body in equilibrium takes the form of a flattened ("oblate") [ellipsoid](/wiki/Ellipsoid "Ellipsoid") of revolution, generated by an [ellipse](/wiki/Ellipse "Ellipse") rotated around its minor diameter; a shape which he termed an [oblate spheroid](/wiki/Oblate_spheroid "Oblate spheroid").[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)

In 1669, [Jean Picard](/wiki/Jean_Picard "Jean Picard") found the first accurate and reliable value for the [radius of Earth](/wiki/Earth_radius "Earth radius") as 6,365.6 [kilometres](/wiki/Kilometre "Kilometre").[[10]](#cite_note-gallica-10)[[11]](#cite_note-The_Shape_of_the_Earth-11)
Picard's geodetic observations had been confined to the determination of the magnitude of the Earth considered as a sphere, but the discovery made by [Jean Richer](/wiki/Jean_Richer "Jean Richer") turned the attention of mathematicians to the Earth's deviation from a spherical form.[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-Earth-figure-13) [Christiaan Huygens](/wiki/Christiaan_Huygens "Christiaan Huygens") found out the [centrifugal force](/wiki/Centrifugal_force "Centrifugal force") which explained variations of gravitational acceleration depending on [latitude](/wiki/Latitude "Latitude").[[14]](#cite_note-Perrier-1935-14)

In 1743, [Alexis Clairaut](/wiki/Alexis_Clairaut "Alexis Clairaut") proposed a theorem which suggested that the study of variations in [gravitational acceleration](/wiki/Gravitational_acceleration "Gravitational acceleration") was a way to determine the [figure of the Earth](/wiki/Figure_of_the_Earth "Figure of the Earth"), whose crucial parameter was the [flattening](/wiki/Flattening "Flattening") of the Earth ellipsoid.[[14]](#cite_note-Perrier-1935-14)[[11]](#cite_note-The_Shape_of_the_Earth-11) Towards the end of the 18th century, the geodesists sought to reconcile the values of flattening drawn from the measurements of [meridian arcs](/wiki/Meridian_arc "Meridian arc") with that given by [Clairaut's theorem](/wiki/Clairaut%27s_theorem_(gravity) "Clairaut's theorem (gravity)") drawn from the measurement of [gravity](/wiki/Gravity "Gravity").[[11]](#cite_note-The_Shape_of_the_Earth-11)[[15]](#cite_note-15) The Weights and Measures Commission would, in 1799, adopt a flattening of ⁠1/334⁠ based on analysis by [Pierre-Simon Laplace](/wiki/Pierre-Simon_Laplace "Pierre-Simon Laplace") who combined the [arc of Peru](/wiki/French_Geodesic_Mission_to_the_Equator "French Geodesic Mission to the Equator") and the data of the [meridian arc of Delambre and Méchain](/wiki/Arc_measurement_of_Delambre_and_M%C3%A9chain "Arc measurement of Delambre and Méchain").[[16]](#cite_note-Nyblom-2023-16): 3[[16]](#cite_note-Nyblom-2023-16)[[17]](#cite_note-Torge-2016-17)

### Historical Earth ellipsoids

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Earth_ellipsoid&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Historical Earth ellipsoids")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3e/WGS84_mean_Earth_radius.svg/250px-WGS84_mean_Earth_radius.svg.png)](/wiki/File:WGS84_mean_Earth_radius.svg)

Equatorial (a), polar (b) and mean Earth radii as defined in the 1984 [World Geodetic System](/wiki/World_Geodetic_System "World Geodetic System") revision (not to scale)

The reference ellipsoid models listed below have had utility in geodetic work and many are still in use. The older ellipsoids are named for the individual who derived them and the year of development is given. In 1887 the English surveyor Colonel [Alexander Ross Clarke](/wiki/Alexander_Ross_Clarke "Alexander Ross Clarke") CB FRS RE was awarded the Gold Medal of the Royal Society for his work in determining the figure of the Earth. The international ellipsoid was developed by [John Fillmore Hayford](/wiki/John_Fillmore_Hayford "John Fillmore Hayford") in 1910 and adopted by the International Union of Geodesy and Geophysics (IUGG) in 1924, which recommended it for international use.

At the 1967 meeting of the IUGG held in Lucerne, Switzerland, the ellipsoid called GRS-67 ([Geodetic Reference System](/wiki/Geodetic_system "Geodetic system") 1967) in the listing was recommended for adoption. The new ellipsoid was not recommended to replace the International Ellipsoid (1924), but was advocated for use where a greater degree of accuracy is required. It became a part of the GRS-67 which was approved and adopted at the 1971 meeting of the IUGG held in Moscow. It is used in Australia for the Australian Geodetic Datum and in the [South American Datum](/wiki/South_American_Datum "South American Datum") 1969.

The GRS-80 (Geodetic Reference System 1980) as approved and adopted by the IUGG at its Canberra, Australia meeting of 1979 is based on the equatorial radius (semi-major axis of Earth ellipsoid) ![{\displaystyle a}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/ffd2487510aa438433a2579450ab2b3d557e5edc), total mass ![{\displaystyle GM}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/d2d9b5427dc6e6f3c11f13d88a5b637b4bc24585), dynamic form factor ![{\displaystyle J_{2}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/0f9986a8fbfd51097a5ff5e82d3252c9572b5835) and angular velocity of rotation ![{\displaystyle \omega }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/48eff443f9de7a985bb94ca3bde20813ea737be8), making the inverse flattening ![{\displaystyle 1/f}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/e9cd0e60c02ddf533da2abed825389fe5a94b7d0) a derived quantity. The minute difference in ![{\displaystyle 1/f}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/e9cd0e60c02ddf533da2abed825389fe5a94b7d0) seen between GRS-80 and WGS-84 results from an unintentional truncation in the latter's defining constants: while the WGS-84 was designed to adhere closely to the GRS-80, incidentally the WGS-84 derived flattening turned out to differ slightly from the GRS-80 flattening because the normalized second degree zonal harmonic gravitational coefficient, that was derived from the GRS-80 value for ![{\displaystyle J_{2}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/0f9986a8fbfd51097a5ff5e82d3252c9572b5835), was truncated to eight significant digits in the normalization process.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

An ellipsoidal model describes only the ellipsoid's geometry and a [normal gravity](/wiki/Normal_gravity "Normal gravity") field formula to go with it. Commonly an ellipsoidal model is part of a more encompassing [geodetic datum](/wiki/Datum_(geodesy) "Datum (geodesy)"). For example, the older ED-50 ([European Datum 1950](/wiki/ED50 "ED50")) is based on the Hayford or [International Ellipsoid](/wiki/International_Ellipsoid "International Ellipsoid"). WGS-84 is peculiar in that the same name is used for both the complete geodetic reference system and its component ellipsoidal model. Nevertheless, the two concepts—ellipsoidal model and geodetic reference system—remain distinct.

Note that the same ellipsoid may be known by different names. It is best to mention the defining constants for unambiguous identification.
